moveAnnotations.annotation: We're playing the Sodium Attack, an offbeat opening that immediately puts your opponent on unfamiliar ground. Moving the knight to a3 (instead of the usual f3) looks awkward, but the idea is to reroute it to c4 where it pressures the center and d6. This gambit-style opening sacrifices conventional development for practical piece placement and quick tactical opportunities—it's great for surprising opponents who haven't prepared against it.

Black claims space in the center with the standard e5 pawn push, establishing a foothold on d4 and e4. This is a natural response that gives Black a solid pawn center, though it also commits the e-pawn early and leaves the d5 square vulnerable. We need to be alert to how White can exploit this central tension.

Now we see the point of Na3—our knight reaches c4 in just two moves, where it eyes the d6 square and puts pressure on Black's e5 pawn. The knight on c4 is actively placed and coordinates well with our later development. Notice how c4 controls key squares (d6, e5, and importantly the d4 square) while staying flexible for our f3 and e4 pawn push.

Black defends the e5 pawn and develops a piece naturally. The knight on c6 also supports potential central play (like d4 ideas) and prepares to challenge our c4 knight. This is solid play from Black, getting a piece out with purpose—they're not just passively accepting our space advantage.

We push through with e4, building a strong pawn center and supporting our c4 knight's pressure on d6 and e5. The e4 pawn controls d5 and f5, restricting Black's options and committing to a space-gaining setup. By playing e4 now, we're gambling that Black's position will become cramped despite having an e5 pawn—the real battle is about what we'll achieve with our active pieces.

Black strikes back immediately with a sharp counterattack, challenging our center and opening lines for the bishop on f8. The move f5 attacks our e4 pawn and aims to destabilize our space advantage, though it weakens the kingside—this is a gambit-like response to our gambit. We now face a sharp middlegame where piece activity and tactics will matter more than positional markers.
